caveats: a: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was found for compost manufacturing.
a: The frozen compensation ratio is for ancestor NAICS 3253 and may not resemble labor intensity at compost sites.
rho: Implementation evidence specific to independent LMM compost producers was not available.
rho: Facility permitting and local nuisance constraints vary widely.

e: Facility counts and firm counts differ because operators may own multiple sites.

s5: Asset purchases or permit transfers may not represent control transfers of going concerns.

q: Revenue mix between tipping fees and compost sales differs materially by facility.

d5: Constant-quality service demand is difficult to separate from changes in feedstock mix and contamination standards.
o: The estimate is for the screened external-service basket, not all composting.
o: Distributed and on-site technologies could reduce outsourced volumes faster than expected.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring tipping or processing fees from waste generators and haulers, supplemented by sales of compost to agriculture, landscaping, construction, and soil-restoration customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Municipal or captive on-site composting, hobby operations, pure hauling, anaerobic digestion without compost production, and producers without recurring external customers.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market compost producers that repeatedly accept organic feedstock from external customers and sell finished compost or soil amendments.

a.bridge: BLS describes chemical-manufacturing production work as a major occupational group, while O*NET identifies production-manager tasks such as scheduling, reports, records, quality-control analysis, inventory, and compliance. I infer that AI can substantially assist the information-handling subset but not equipment operation, feedstock handling, sampling, maintenance, or accountable judgment; ancestor-industry occupational mix may overstate technical and laboratory work relative to composting.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was found for compost manufacturing.
The frozen compensation ratio is for ancestor NAICS 3253 and may not resemble labor intensity at compost sites.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from 15-25 independent compost facilities, mapped to time studies for dispatch, scale-house, sales, compliance, and production roles, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The most exposed wage-weighted work is office administration, dispatch and scheduling, customer service, reporting, contamination triage support, and compliance documentation. Physical receiving, windrow or vessel operation, turning, screening, loading, maintenance, and site supervision remain predominantly embodied work.

d5.bridge: EPA reports that food is 24% of landfilled municipal solid waste and attributes 58% of landfill methane emissions to wasted food, while federal strategy promotes organics recycling and California law targeted a 75% reduction in organic-waste disposal from 2014 levels by 2025. I translate these policy and waste-flow pressures into positive five-year demand for commercial organics processing; the proxy may overstate compost demand because prevention, animal feed, anaerobic digestion, and land application compete for the same stream.
caveats: EPA national waste data are not a forecast for NAICS 325315.
Constant-quality service demand is difficult to separate from changes in feedstock mix and contamination standards.
d5.change_evidence: State-by-state permitted compost capacity, contracted inbound tonnage, five-year organics-diversion mandates, and constant-price tipping-volume forecasts would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Landfill-diversion mandates, methane policy, and the large remaining organics stream support volume growth, while waste prevention, permitting delays, contamination, and rival pathways cap the outlook.

caveats: The assigned firm-count input is missing and must not be inferred.
Facility counts and firm counts differ because operators may own multiple sites.
e.change_evidence: A national facility census classifying ownership, independence, revenue, EBITDA, customer mix, and whether tipping services are recurring would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Many commercial compost producers fit the recurring external-service lens through tipping contracts and repeat product sales, but municipal, captive, farm-scale, community, and integrated waste-company facilities do not. Eligibility is therefore substantial but far from universal.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 3253: labor compensation (L02) $4,197.8M / sectoral output (T30) $42,236.2M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $1,498,136 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 32531); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate is for the screened external-service basket, not all composting.
Distributed and on-site technologies could reduce outsourced volumes faster than expected.
o.change_evidence: Customer surveys quantifying planned on-site composting, prevention, digestion, and self-haul adoption, paired with processor retention and closure data, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Even with better prevention and on-site options, most commercial-scale organic residuals still require a permitted operator to receive, control, process, test, and market material. Software can improve coordination but cannot substitute for land, equipment, biological processing, nuisance control, and regulatory accountability.

caveats: No representative contract dataset was available.
Revenue mix between tipping fees and compost sales differs materially by facility.
q.change_evidence: A sample of customer contracts showing term, indexation, open-book provisions, rebid frequency, and realized gross-margin changes after productivity projects would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Fixed tipping fees and product prices should allow meaningful retention between renewals, particularly when disposal capacity and permits are locally constrained. Competitive rebids, indexed municipal contracts, powerful haulers, and commoditized compost sales should share part of savings with customers over five years.

caveats: Implementation evidence specific to independent LMM compost producers was not available.
Facility permitting and local nuisance constraints vary widely.
rho.change_evidence: Documented before-and-after labor hours and error rates from multi-site compost operators deploying AI-enabled dispatch, scale-house, contamination inspection, and compliance systems would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Commodity software should make administrative copilots and scheduling achievable within five years, but fragmented site data, variable feedstocks, environmental recordkeeping, quality assurance, and the need for human approval limit implementation. AI does not remove the operational bottleneck of physical processing.

caveats: No observed denominator of eligible LMM compost firms or qualifying control transfers was found.
Asset purchases or permit transfers may not represent control transfers of going concerns.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, state permit ownership changes, and interviews with waste-sector brokers focused on independent compost operators would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: The base case reflects ordinary private-company succession and strategic sale activity over five years. Permits, land, feedstock relationships, and local contracts make viable operations transferable, but many sites are municipal, captive, or family-controlled and observed compost-specific control-transfer data were unavailable.

principal_driver: Policy-backed diversion of organics into a locally constrained, operator-required physical process.
principal_weakness: No defensible eligible-firm denominator or compost-specific control-transfer history, compounded by limited direct task data.
